mnemonics.title: M1O Pattern for Thread Models
mnemonics.content: MANY-TO-ONE: M2O = MANY USER → ONE KERNEL - Think 'Many people, one door' - they must queue up (block together)

mnemonics.title: O1O Pattern
mnemonics.content: ONE-TO-ONE: O1O = ONE USER → ONE KERNEL - Think 'One person, one passport' - each has their own identity and can move independently

mnemonics.title: M2M Pattern
mnemonics.content: MANY-TO-MANY: M2M = MANY USER ↔ MANY KERNEL - Think 'Many tellers, many customers' - flexible queue management

acronyms.usage: A kernel-scheduled entity in Solaris that acts as a virtual processor for user threads
A standardized thread API used in UNIX/Linux systems for portable multithreading
Executes Java bytecode; modern JVM uses One-to-One model with native OS threads

visualTips.visualization: Imagine ONE elevator (kernel thread) serving MULTIPLE floors (user threads). If the elevator stops (blocks), no one moves.
Imagine MULTIPLE dedicated elevators, one for each person. Everyone moves independently without waiting.
Imagine a bank with MULTIPLE tellers (kernel threads) serving MULTIPLE customers (user threads). Flexible and efficient.

analogies.analogy: Many-to-One is like a single bus carrying all passengers - if the bus breaks, everyone stops. One-to-One is like everyone having their own car. Many-to-Many is like a rideshare pool with multiple cars serving multiple passengers.

analogies.concept: Kernel vs User Threads as Teachers and Students
analogies.analogy: User threads are students wanting to learn (execute). Kernel threads are actual classrooms (CPU cores). Many-to-One is one classroom for all students. One-to-One is one classroom per student. Many-to-Many is flexible classroom allocation.
